metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: AC3's grep oracle (`grep -ni "advisory" src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_coach.md`) is a substring false-positive: it will match any occurrence of the word 'advisory' in the file — including legitimate uses such as the decision tag label value (e.g., `decision="advisory"`) or inline examples — producing spurious failures that block a correct implementation, or requiring the executor to interpret the prose qualifier 'no hits framing the input set as advisory-only' subjectively rather than mechanically.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3: 'grep -ni "advisory" src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_coach.md returns no hits framing the input set as advisory-only'
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the single grep with two commands:
1. `grep -niP '\badvisory\s+(findings|only|inputs?)\b' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_coach.md` → must return no hits (rejects advisory-only framing)
2. `grep -ni 'surviving findings' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_coach.md` → must return at least one hit (confirms neutral framing is present)
The unit test for the header text remains as the primary assertion; the greps are sanity guards only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `review_kernel/coach.py:render_coach_notes` itself gains the decision-map parameter or whether a passes.py wrapper replaces the alias, and add the exact change site to the What section.
evidence: Plan: 'passes.render_coach_notes gains an optional decision map (finding_ref -> "block"|"advisory"); both callers … pass it, so every coaching entry carries `decision`.'
passes.py:75: `render_coach_notes = _coach.render_coach_notes` — it is a direct alias, not a wrapper.
review_kernel/coach.py:162: `def render_coach_notes(raw_notes: list[dict[str, Any]], registry: MoveRegistry) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:` — two parameters only, no decision map.
The What section does not name review_kernel/coach.py:render_coach_notes as a change site.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'passes.render_coach_notes gains an optional decision map (finding_ref -> "block"|"advisory"); both callers (the bespoke pass4 coach in passes.py and `plan_review_coach`) pass it, so every coaching entry carries `decision`.' But `passes.render_coach_notes` is a thin alias of `_coach.render_coach_notes` (review_kernel/coach.py:162) — a simple `(raw_notes, registry) -> list` function with no decision-map parameter. The plan never lists `review_kernel/coach.py render_coach_notes` as a change site, so the kernel function must be modified but is absent from the What section.
findings.location: What section / passes.py:75, review_kernel/coach.py:162-185
scenarios: Implementation adds the decision-map parameter to passes.render_coach_notes (intending a wrapper), but because it is an alias the assignment silently updates to the new kernel function — meaning the kernel function must be the actual change site.

If a wrapper is added in passes.py instead of modifying the kernel, the bespoke `pass4_coach` path (which calls review_kernel.coach → render_coach_notes directly) bypasses the wrapper and never emits decision tags.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the plan explicitly calls out that all three YAML changes (coach_inputs `blocking:` bind, coach_gate `when:`, coach_notes `surviving:`) must be applied identically to BOTH the `code_grounded` then-arm (~lines 174-226) AND the else-arm (~lines 252-304) of plan-review.yaml.
evidence: YAML lines 174-180: coach_inputs in the code_grounded then-arm binds only `surviving: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.surfaced }}`
YAML lines 252-258: identical coach_inputs in the else-arm also binds only `surviving: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.surfaced }}`

The plan states changes as if there is a single coach_inputs step, e.g. 'add blocking: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.blocking }} to the coach_inputs step's with:' — no mention of the identical change needed in the else-arm
findings.finding: The plan-review.yaml workflow contains TWO structurally-identical coach_inputs/coach_gate/coach_notes blocks — one inside the `code_grounded` then-arm (lines ~174-226) and an identical copy inside the else-arm (lines ~252-304). The plan's three YAML changes (add `blocking:` to coach_inputs, change coach_gate `when:`, change coach_notes `surviving:`) must be applied to BOTH arms, but the plan only describes them once without acknowledging the duplication.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml, lines 174-226 (then-arm) and 252-304 (else-arm); plan section 'What' / 'plan-review.yaml' bullet
scenarios: An implementer patches only one arm; blocking-finding coaching works for one verify-path but not the other, producing asymmetric behavior that is invisible in most test runs which don't exercise both branches
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the 'What / plan-review.yaml' bullet: 'Apply all three changes in BOTH arms of verify_branch (the code_grounded then-arm and else-arm are structurally identical; each contains its own coach_inputs / coach_gate / coach_notes block).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly call out in the plan that BOTH the agentic (then:) and single-turn (else:) `verify_branch` arms contain duplicate `coach_inputs`/`coach_gate`/`coach_notes` steps, and that all six step entries must be updated.
evidence: plan-review.yaml lines 174-180: agentic arm coach_inputs step with surviving: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.surfaced }}
plan-review.yaml lines 252-258: single-turn arm coach_inputs step — identical structure, identical missing blocking input
Plan text: 'plan-review.yaml: add blocking: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.blocking }} to the coach_inputs step's with:' — uses singular 'step' with no mention of the duplication
YAML comment at line 259: identical comment to line 181, confirming the structural duplication
findings.finding: The workflow YAML (plan-review.yaml) has TWO duplicate structural arms — the `code_grounded` agentic arm (then:) and the single-turn arm (else:) — each containing their own `coach_inputs`, `coach_gate`, and `coach_notes` steps. The plan's YAML change instructions name the step ids only once without calling out that both arms must be updated, creating a risk that only one arm receives the widening.
findings.location: plan-review.yaml: lines 174-211 (agentic arm) and lines 252-304 (single-turn arm); plan 'What' section: 'plan-review.yaml: add blocking: … to the coach_inputs step's with:; change coach_gate's when:; change coach_notes's surviving: binding'
scenarios: Implementer updates only the agentic arm (then:); the single-turn arm (else:, the cheaper non-code-grounded path) still excludes blocking findings from coaching
A non-code-grounded plan (the majority of tickets) takes the else: arm and blocking findings receive no coaching despite the plan's stated goal
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'Note: plan-review.yaml has two identical coach_inputs/coach_gate/coach_notes sequences (one inside verify_branch.then, one inside verify_branch.else); all changes must be applied to both arms.'

findings.finding: plan-review.yaml duplicates the coach_inputs + coach_gate steps verbatim in BOTH arms of the verify_branch (agentic arm lines 174-226, non-agentic arm lines 253-304). The plan says 'update the coach_gate branch when: condition' (singular) and 'widen the coach_inputs binding', but neither the What section nor the YAML edit description mentions that there are TWO copies to update. An implementer updating only the first (agentic) arm leaves the non-agentic arm with the old advisory-only behavior.
findings.location: What section (workflow path bullet) / src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml lines 174-226 and 253-304

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that `pass4_coach` (passes.py:842) is a test-utility function not on the live workflow path, and that the live widening is entirely in `plan_review_coach` (workflow_ops.py:475) — `pass4_coach` may optionally be widened for test-parity but is not the live seam.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py:842: `def pass4_coach(runner, cfg, *, plan, surviving, move_registry)` — accepts only `surviving`, no `blocking`
No production call site for pass4_coach found outside of tests/unit/test_plan_review.py:577
The workflow gate uses plan_review_coach (workflow_ops.py:475) which calls passes.render_coach_notes directly — a separate code path from pass4_coach
Plan says 'the bespoke pass-4 invocation in passes.py is widened' implying this is part of the live path widening, but it is test-only infrastructure
findings.finding: The plan says 'the bespoke pass-4 invocation in passes.py is widened from advisory-only to blocking+advisory the same way' — but `pass4_coach` in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py:842` is not called anywhere in the live workflow path. The workflow exclusively uses the `plan_review_coach` workflow op (workflow_ops.py:475); `pass4_coach` is only called from unit tests. Widening `pass4_coach` does NOT widen the live path, and the plan conflates the two as if they were the same change.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py:842; plan section 'What' bullet on passes.py
scenarios: Implementer widens pass4_coach but misses the equivalent change in plan_review_coach (workflow_ops.py), so the live workflow path still only coaches surviving advisory findings and the AC oracles (which test the op, not pass4_coach) fail

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the resolution rule for `decision` when a coaching note's `finding_refs` spans both blocking and advisory findings (e.g. 'block takes priority over advisory').
evidence: Plan: 'render_coach_notes (src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:162) gains an optional decision map (finding_ref -> "block"|"advisory")'
Existing render_coach_notes builds one output entry per raw note; a note can have finding_refs=['f1','f2'] where f1='block' and f2='advisory'
No rule is stated for resolving conflicting decisions across a note's finding_refs — 'first wins', 'most severe wins', or 'block wins' are all plausible but none is specified
findings.finding: The `render_coach_notes` function is specified to gain an optional `decision` map (`finding_ref -> 'block'|'advisory'`), but the plan never defines the population rule when a coaching note's `finding_refs` list spans findings with different decisions (one blocking, one advisory). The output `decision` field value is ambiguous for mixed-refs notes.
findings.location: What section: 'Decision tag: passes.render_coach_notes … both callers … pass it, so every coaching entry carries decision'
scenarios: A move pick references both a blocking and an advisory finding (e.g. finding_refs=['block-f1', 'advisory-f2']); the rendered coaching entry's decision field is undefined
A downstream consumer (CLI renderer, sidecar) keying off decision='block' to apply special formatting gets inconsistent results
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Decision tag' bullet: 'When a note's finding_refs spans mixed decisions, use the most severe: "block" supersedes "advisory".'

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).

verify command `grep -niP "advisory\s+(findings|only)" src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_coach.md` inspects output with `grep` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan exactly how `plan_review_coach` builds the `finding_ref -> decision` map from its `blocking` and `surfaced` inputs before passing it to `render_coach_notes` (e.g., index by finding `id` field, or by position in the coachable list).
evidence: Plan: 'passes.render_coach_notes (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py) gains an optional decision map (finding_ref -> "block"|"advisory"); both callers ... pass it'
plan_review_coach receives blocking and surfaced as separate lists (workflow_ops.py:487-491) but the plan does not specify the step that builds the decision map from these
render_coach_notes currently takes only (raw_notes, registry) — coach.py:162
findings.finding: The plan says `render_coach_notes` gains an optional decision map parameter so every coaching entry carries `decision`, but the workflow execution path does not specify WHERE and HOW this decision map is derived inside `plan_review_coach` — the op receives `blocking` and `surfaced` as separate lists but the plan is silent on the derivation of a `finding_ref -> 'block'|'advisory'` map from them before passing it to `render_coach_notes`.
findings.location: What section — 'Decision tag' bullet; workflow_ops.py:475 (plan_review_coach op)
scenarios: Implementer adds the decision parameter to render_coach_notes but cannot derive it inside plan_review_coach because finding_refs in raw_notes are free-form strings that may not directly match the ids in the blocking/surfaced lists — the mapping logic is non-trivial

findings.finding: The plan specifies that `render_coach_notes` gains an optional `decision map (finding_ref -> "block"|"advisory")` so every coaching entry carries `decision`, but the signature extension, the lookup semantics for multi-ref notes (a note whose `finding_refs` spans refs from both buckets), and the fallback value when a ref is absent from the map are never defined.
findings.location: What section / Decision tag bullet: 'passes.render_coach_notes (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py) gains an optional decision map'

findings.finding: `pass4_coach` in `passes.py` (lines 842–871) currently accepts only `surviving: list[dict]`. The plan says 'The bespoke pass4 invocation in passes.py is widened from advisory-only to blocking + advisory the same way' — but does not enumerate changing the function signature to accept `blocking` as a separate parameter, nor specify how the combined list is passed to `review_kernel.coach()` whose first parameter is also named `surviving`.
findings.location: What / Decision tag bullet; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py:842–871

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC oracle for the bespoke pass-4 path in passes.py: a unit test calling the passes.py coach directly with a synthetic verdict containing a blocking finding and asserting the returned coaching entry carries decision='block' and guide_url.
evidence: Plan: 'The bespoke pass-4 invocation in passes.py is widened from advisory-only to blocking + advisory the same way.'
AC1 oracle: 'new unit test asserting a synthetic verdict with 1 block + 1 advisory yields 2 coaching entries' — does not name passes.py as the exercised path
AC2 oracle: explicitly names 'tests/unit/workflow/' and StepContext stubs, confirming it targets the workflow op path, not passes.py
ABSENCE: no AC says 'the bespoke pass-4 coach in passes.py yields coaching entries for blocking findings'
findings.finding: The bespoke pass-4 coach path in passes.py (widened from advisory-only to blocking+advisory, with decision map added to render_coach_notes) is a described implementation step but no AC oracle explicitly exercises this code path in isolation. AC1's oracle (`pytest -k 'coach'`) may or may not cover it depending on which test file is written, and AC2 explicitly covers the workflow op path. If a future test only exercises the workflow op path (plan_review_coach), the passes.py bespoke path could regress silently.
findings.location: What section / passes.py bespoke path + AC1 oracle
scenarios: An executor writes AC1's test against plan_review_coach (the workflow op), AC2 covers the workflow YAML path, and the passes.py widening is never independently exercised — a bug in the passes.py decision-map wiring goes undetected.
CI is green because the workflow-op path works, but the standalone pass-4 review invocation (which uses passes.py directly) silently drops coaching on blocking findings.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Unit test directly invoking the bespoke pass-4 coach in passes.py (not via workflow ops) with a verdict containing 1 blocking + 1 advisory finding asserts 2 coaching entries where the blocking entry carries decision="block" and a non-None guide_url; `python -m pytest tests/unit/passes/ -k "pass4_coach" -x` green.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the `coach_listing` header change in the shared kernel (`review_kernel/coach.py:123`) is intended to also affect code-review; if plan-review-only, apply the neutralised header via a plan-review-specific wrapper or a caller-supplied `header=` parameter rather than mutating the shared kernel string.
evidence: Plan says: 'the hardcoded listing header `## Surviving advisory findings (by id)` in src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:123 becomes decision-neutral'
grep: `code_review/workflow_ops.py:473: instructions = review_kernel.coach_listing(surfaced, applicable)` — code-review calls the same shared function
The plan's Scope section states OUT: 'any change to ... the sidecar schema beyond the optional decision field' but does NOT scope out code-review; the shared kernel change bleeds into it
findings.finding: The plan's header-neutralization target (`'## Surviving advisory findings (by id)'` in `review_kernel/coach.py:123`) is inside a SHARED KERNEL function (`coach_listing`) that is also consumed by code-review (`code_review/workflow_ops.py:473`). Changing it for the plan-review-only goal of decision-neutral wording will silently alter the instruction string fed to code-review's coach LLM step — an unscoped side-effect on a gate the plan explicitly leaves OUT of scope.
findings.location: What section → 'Prompt + header' bullet; src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:123; src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:473
scenarios: Code-review coach LLM receives a neutralised header it was never authored against; if the code-review coach prompt or any downstream test asserts the exact header text it will silently diverge.
Any existing test that fixture-checks the `coach_listing` output string (including code-review unit tests) will fail.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an optional `header: str = 'Surviving advisory findings (by id)'` parameter to `coach_listing`; plan-review passes `'Surviving findings (by id)'`; code-review passes nothing (existing default preserved). This keeps the shared kernel backward-compatible.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Instead of adding `decision_map` to the shared-kernel `render_coach_notes`, apply the `decision` tag in the plan-review-specific post-render step (e.g., annotate each coaching entry with `decision` in `plan_review_coach` or `finalize_verdict`) so the shared kernel API stays lean.
evidence: Plan says: '`passes.render_coach_notes` … gains an optional decision map (finding_ref -> "block"|"advisory"); both callers (the bespoke pass4 coach in passes.py and `plan_review_coach`) pass it'
grep shows render_coach_notes call-sites: plan_review/workflow_ops.py:500, code_review/workflow_ops.py:501 — code-review is a third call-site that will NOT pass decision_map
Rule-of-Three: 2 plan-review call-sites within the same gate do not satisfy the ≥3 independent consumers bar for a shared kernel parameter
findings.finding: The optional `decision_map` parameter proposed for the shared-kernel `render_coach_notes` (in `review_kernel/coach.py`) is consumed by only plan-review callers (2 call-sites: `passes.py` bespoke path and `workflow_ops.py:500`); the code-review call-site (`code_review/workflow_ops.py:501`) will not pass it. Rule of Three: 2 call-sites in a single gate do not justify adding a new optional parameter to a shared kernel function. The `decision` tag could instead be applied post-render in plan-review-specific code (e.g., inside `plan_review_coach` op or `finalize_verdict`) without touching the shared kernel.
findings.location: What section → 'Decision tag' bullet; src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py (render_coach_notes); src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:500; src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:501
scenarios: Shared kernel accumulates plan-review-specific parameters over time; code-review callers must know to ignore the optional param; future gate authors see a confusing kernel API.
If a future code-review feature adds decision tagging, the param name/shape may already be locked to plan-review semantics.
findings.suggested_fix: In `plan_review_coach` (workflow_ops.py), after `coaching = passes.render_coach_notes(...)`, iterate `coaching` and set `entry['decision'] = 'block' if entry['finding_refs'] intersects blocking_ids else 'advisory'`. No kernel change required.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:501` (the `code_review_coach` call to `review_kernel.render_coach_notes`) as an unenumerated consumer of the kernel function's signature change, and confirm the new `decision` parameter is keyword-only with a default of `None` to preserve backward compatibility.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:501: `coaching = review_kernel.render_coach_notes(raw_notes, applicable)`

Plan says: 'passes.render_coach_notes (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py) gains an optional decision map' — render_coach_notes is a re-export of the kernel function, so the kernel function itself changes
code_review_coach calls it without a decision map — the call site is unenumerated
findings.finding: The plan adds an optional `decision` parameter to `passes.render_coach_notes` (which is an alias of `review_kernel.coach.render_coach_notes`). This is a KERNEL function also called by `code_review_coach` in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:501` (`coaching = review_kernel.render_coach_notes(raw_notes, applicable)`). This consumer is not enumerated in the plan's Scope or What sections.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:501; plan section 'What' bullet on render_coach_notes
scenarios: If the signature change is backward-compatible (optional kwarg), code_review_coach continues working. But if the plan's test assertions depend on the decision field appearing only on plan-review coaching entries, a future caller inadvertently passing decision to code_review_coach could produce unexpected output.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:473` (code_review_coach_inputs calling `review_kernel.coach_listing`) as an unenumerated consumer of the header rename, and verify that renaming the header does not break the code-review coach prompt or its tests.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:473: `instructions = review_kernel.coach_listing(surfaced, applicable)` — passes the generated instructions (including the header) to code-review's coach step
Plan scope bullet: 'OUT: any change to move-registry contents' — but the header rename is in scope and affects code-review
findings.finding: The plan renames the hardcoded header string in `coach_listing` (`review_kernel/coach.py:123`) from `'## Surviving advisory findings (by id)'` to a decision-neutral string. `coach_listing` is called from `code_review_coach_inputs` in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:473` (`instructions = review_kernel.coach_listing(surfaced, applicable)`), so the header rename also changes the instructions fed to the code-review coach prompt. This is an unenumerated side-effect on the code-review gate.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:473; src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:123; plan section 'What' / 'Prompt + header' bullet
scenarios: Code-review prompt regression if the code-review coach prompt or its test assertions reference the exact header string; unintended behavior change in code-review coaching without any test coverage for the header change on that path

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `blocking` to the `plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json` properties alongside `surviving`, mirroring the output schema update that is already called out.
evidence: plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json properties: only {ticket_id, surviving} — no `blocking` field
Plan states: 'The op's output schema … is CLOSED … add findings … to its properties' — explicitly addresses output schema
Plan is silent on the input schema; 'additionalProperties: true' on the input schema means the engine won't reject an extra `blocking` field, but schema documentation is incomplete
findings.finding: The plan specifies adding `blocking` as a new input to the `plan_review_coach_inputs` op but does not mention updating the INPUT schema (`plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json`) which currently only declares `ticket_id` and `surviving`. The output schema update is called out explicitly, but the input schema is not.
findings.location: src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json; plan 'What' section: 'plan_review_coach_inputs (workflow_ops.py:360): accept a new blocking input'
scenarios: A schema-validation tool or documentation generator reports `blocking` as an undeclared input
A future schema-tightening pass (e.g. adding additionalProperties: false to the input schema) silently drops the blocking field
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the input schema: `"blocking": { "type": "array", "description": "The blocking findings (the decide op's blocking partition) prepended to coachable." }`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the new `blocking` input to `plan_review_coach_inputs` is **optional** (defaulting to an empty list `[]`) so existing callers remain unbroken without a migration step, OR explicitly enumerate all callers that must be updated and add that to Scope/AC.
evidence: 'accept a new `blocking` input, compute `coachable = blocking + surviving`' — no optionality or default specified
The YAML change is the only coordinated caller mentioned; no mention of existing tests or other callers needing updates
T4 requires an expand-contract sequence or rollback path for changes consumers rely on; silently breaking existing op callers with no migration note is the failure mode here
findings.finding: The plan adds a new `blocking` input to the `plan_review_coach_inputs` op but does not specify whether this input is optional (defaulting to an empty list) or required. If required, any existing caller — including existing unit tests for that op — that does not supply `blocking` would break with no migration path described.
findings.location: What section — `plan_review_coach_inputs` (workflow_ops.py:360): 'accept a new `blocking` input'
scenarios: Existing unit tests for `plan_review_coach_inputs` that construct a StepContext without a `blocking` key raise a KeyError or schema-validation error, causing the test suite to break before the new tests are even added.
A second YAML workflow that calls `plan_review_coach_inputs` without the new field silently errors at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, amend the description to: 'accept a new optional `blocking` input (default `[]`), compute `coachable = blocking + surviving`…'. This makes the change purely additive and eliminates the need for a rollback path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CLAUDE.md or design-doc note (or lightweight ADR) capturing the coaching pipeline contract extension: that coaching entries now include blocking findings, carry a `decision` field (`'block'|'advisory'`), and that `findings` replaces the `surviving` binding as the unified input to coach steps.
evidence: 'Decision tag: passes.render_coach_notes ... gains an optional decision map (finding_ref -> "block"|"advisory")' — new output contract field
'Sidecar slim: build_payload ... add decision to that list so the tag survives persistence' — field now part of persisted contract
No mention of CLAUDE.md, ADR, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
T5e(c): 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / CLAUDE.md / design-doc update'
findings.finding: The plan extends the coaching pipeline's output contract — adding a `decision` field to coaching entries and making blocking findings first-class coached output — but includes no update to CLAUDE.md, an ADR, or any design doc capturing this new contract. Future developers and agent consumers parsing coaching output need a stable reference for what fields to expect and why blocking findings are now included.
findings.location: ## What (overall); Scope section; sidecar slim bullet; orchestrator.py:515-530 bullet
scenarios: A new developer extends the coaching pipeline and doesn't know `decision` is an expected field, omitting it and breaking downstream consumers
An agent parsing coaching output is unaware that blocking entries are now present and misinterprets them as advisory
The sidecar schema evolves and the `decision` field is dropped without recognizing it's a contract field
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short section to CLAUDE.md (or a `docs/adr/coaching-pipeline-blocking-extension.md`) describing: (1) coaching now covers both blocking and advisory findings, blocking-first; (2) every coaching entry carries `decision: 'block' | 'advisory'`; (3) the `findings` output from `plan_review_coach_inputs` is the canonical union fed to all downstream coach steps. This can be 5–10 lines.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC or plan prose specifying the CLI fallback render for a blocking finding when (a) no coaching entry exists for it, (b) guide_url is absent, and (c) the coach step errored — and extend AC-6's oracle to cover at least the no-coaching-entry case.
evidence: AC-6: 'CLI rendering unit test asserting the rendered text contains the guide_url for a blocking finding' — covers only the success case
Plan says 'moves come from the locked registry' but does not specify fallback when no move matches a blocking finding criterion
ABSENCE: no plan prose addresses CLI output when coaching entries are absent or incomplete for a blocking finding
findings.finding: The CLI non-happy-path for missing or failed coaching on a blocking finding is unspecified. AC-6 only covers the happy path (guide_url present); no AC or plan prose describes what `rebar review-plan` renders when a blocking finding has no coaching entry (no registry match), when guide_url is absent/None, or when the coach LLM step errored and produced no coaching output for that finding.
findings.location: AC-6 / What section ('CLI: rebar review-plan renders the coaching line + guide link under blocking findings') / src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py
scenarios: Agent sees a BLOCK verdict; coach LLM call times out; CLI silently shows blocking finding with no coaching line — agent has no remediation guidance and no indication the coaching pipeline failed
Blocking finding maps to a criterion with no registry entry; CLI renders nothing under the block with no error or placeholder
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fallback: if no coaching entry exists for a blocking finding, the CLI renders the finding without a coaching line but emits a warning (e.g., '(no coaching available for this finding)'). Extend AC-6: 'when no coaching entry is present for a blocking finding, the rendered text omits the coaching line without raising an error.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a guard in `plan_review_coach_inputs` for a None/missing `blocking` input (treat as empty list) and add at least one log/warning when the `coachable` list is non-empty but coaching entries are not generated, so failures in the new path are observable at runtime.
evidence: Plan states 'compute coachable = blocking + surviving' but specifies no guard for None/missing blocking input
The gate when-condition changes to `${{ steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings }}` — if coach_inputs errors or emits empty findings due to a bug in the new path, the branch silently skips
ABSENCE: no mention of logging, metrics, or alerts for the new blocking-findings-to-coaching path
Plan explicitly scopes OUT cross-finding conflict detection and registry changes but says nothing about error handling for the new input
findings.finding: The new `coach_inputs` execution path (computing `coachable = blocking + surviving`) has no specified error handling or observability instrumentation. If the `blocking` input is None, malformed, or the concatenation raises, the gate condition change means the coaching branch may silently not fire even when blocking findings exist — with no metric, log, or alert to surface the failure.
findings.location: What section / workflow_ops.py:360 (`plan_review_coach_inputs`) / plan-review.yaml (`coach_gate` when-condition change)
scenarios: blocking input is None (e.g., upstream decide step emits no blocking key); coachable silently becomes surviving-only; blocking findings get no coaching with no observable signal
coach_inputs op raises an unhandled exception on the new blocking input; workflow swallows it; coaching branch skips entirely; downstream consumers see no coaching entries for blocks
findings.suggested_fix: In workflow_ops.py:360, default `blocking` to `[]` if absent: `blocking = ctx.inputs.get('blocking') or []`; add a `logger.warning` if `coachable` is non-empty but `instructions`/`triggers` are empty after processing. Document this guard in the plan's What section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether widening `passes.pass4_coach` also requires updating `review_kernel.coach()` to accept a combined blocking+advisory list, and add `review_kernel/coach.py` to the explicit change-site list if so.
evidence: Plan: 'The bespoke pass-4 invocation in passes.py is widened from advisory-only to blocking + advisory the same way.'
passes.pass4_coach calls `review_kernel.coach(surviving, move_registry, pick=_pick, active_triggers=triggers)` — `surviving` is the only findings parameter.
review_kernel/coach.py:195: `def coach(surviving: list[...], registry: ..., *, pick: ..., active_triggers: ...)` — no `blocking` parameter and no combined-list input.
The plan does not list `review_kernel.coach` as a change site anywhere in the What or Scope sections.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The bespoke pass-4 invocation in passes.py is widened from advisory-only to blocking + advisory the same way,' implying that widening `pass4_coach` in passes.py alone suffices. But `passes.pass4_coach` delegates immediately to `review_kernel.coach(surviving, ...)`, and the kernel `coach()` function signature itself accepts only `surviving` (not a combined blocking+advisory list). The plan never names `review_kernel.coach` as a change site, so widening `pass4_coach` without also touching the kernel entry-point leaves the bespoke path untouched.
findings.location: What section / passes.py:842-871, review_kernel/coach.py:195-216
scenarios: The `pass4_coach` bespoke path (used by drift_refresh and any non-workflow caller) never gets coaching on blocking findings because the kernel `coach()` still gates on `surviving` only.
The plan's AC-1 asserts 'a synthetic verdict with 1 block + 1 advisory yields 2 coaching entries' — if the bespoke path is tested, it will produce only 1 entry unless the kernel is also widened.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'review_kernel.coach() (review_kernel/coach.py:195): accept an optional `blocking` parameter (defaulting to []) and combine it with `surviving` before the applicability filter, so the kernel entry-point mirrors the coach_inputs widening.' Alternatively, specify that the bespoke `pass4_coach` combines the lists before calling the unchanged kernel.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the CLI description: coaching entries render as a flat global list (not under each blocking finding), and confirm whether AC-6's oracle should additionally assert the guide_url appears for a coach entry whose finding_refs references a blocking finding.
evidence: Plan: 'CLI: `rebar review-plan` renders the coaching line + guide link under each blocking finding the same way it does for advisories'

_render_plan_review_text lines 536-537: iterates `result.get('blocking', [])` printing each finding, then lines 539-542 iterates advisory findings, then lines 551-553 iterates `result.get('coaching', [])` as a flat list — coaching is never nested under individual findings.
The plan proposes no structural change to this rendering loop.
findings.finding: The plan claims the CLI 'renders the coaching line + guide link under each blocking finding.' The actual `_render_plan_review_text` (src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py:524-553) renders all coaching entries as a FLAT global list AFTER iterating blocking and advisory findings — there is no per-finding nesting. No plan change is described to restructure this rendering, so either the plan misstates the intended output format or the AC-6 oracle ('rendered text contains the guide_url for a blocking finding') will pass even with the flat list while the prose description is wrong.
findings.location: What section ('CLI: rebar review-plan renders the coaching line + guide link under each blocking finding') / src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py:536-553
scenarios: An agent reading the plan description expects coaching lines interleaved with blocking finding lines in the CLI output; the actual output has them at the end — confusion or a divergence between the described and delivered behavior.
AC-6's oracle ('rendered text contains the guide_url for a blocking finding') will pass with the flat list, masking the prose/behavior mismatch.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'under each blocking finding' with 'in the shared coaching section (after blocking and advisory findings are listed)' in the What section; if interleaved rendering is actually desired, add an explicit CLI restructuring step to the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that changing the `coach_listing` header in the shared kernel (`coach.py:123`) does not adversely affect other gates' coaching prompts, or parameterise the header string so each caller controls it independently.
evidence: Plan: 'the hardcoded listing header `## Surviving advisory findings (by id)` in src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:123 becomes decision-neutral'
coach_listing is a shared kernel function (rebar.llm.review_kernel.coach) re-exported by passes.py as coach_instructions; it is called by every gate that uses the WS3 coach mechanism
Plan scope does not flag this as a cross-gate mutation
findings.finding: The plan changes `coach_listing` (aliased as `coach_instructions`) in `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:123`, a SHARED kernel function used by all gates — but neither flags this as a cross-gate risk nor states a positive rationale for mutating the shared kernel header rather than parameterising the listing or overriding it in plan-review's call site.
findings.location: What section — 'Prompt + header' bullet; src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:123
scenarios: A future gate (b744) or code-review gate that uses the same kernel coach_listing sees a prompt header change it did not expect, potentially altering LLM behaviour for that gate's advisory-only coaching
findings.suggested_fix: Add an optional `header` parameter to `coach_listing` (defaulting to the current advisory string for backward compatibility) and pass the decision-neutral string only from plan-review's call site, keeping the kernel change non-breaking.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the What section explaining why both `plan_review_coach_inputs` and `plan_review_coach` must be widened symmetrically (triggers must match between what the LLM is offered and what render validates).
evidence: Plan Why: 'a guide link plus a move-shaped nudge on the block itself is the cheapest one-round-remediation lever' — this motivates the feature, not the chosen approach
No statement like 'we widen at both seams rather than [alternative] because [reason]' appears anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for WHY widening at both execution seams (`coach_inputs` op + `plan_review_coach` op) is the right structural choice — the Why section explains the problem, not the approach.
findings.location: Why section; What section
scenarios: An implementer must infer why both ops need to change rather than, e.g., having coach_inputs pass-through the decision tag and letting plan_review_coach derive it

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm in the plan that fixing `coach_listing`'s header at `coach.py:123` resolves the instruction-locality issue for BOTH the workflow path (`plan_review_coach_inputs` via the `passes.coach_instructions` alias) and the bespoke path (`pass4_coach`), so implementers do not introduce a second copy.
evidence: passes.py:74: coach_instructions = _coach.coach_listing
workflow_ops.py:374: instructions = passes.coach_instructions(surviving, applicable) — called with coachable after widening

The LLM prompt will receive blocking findings labelled as 'advisory findings' until coach.py:123 is fixed — but the plan does not confirm that this single fix serves both the workflow AND bespoke paths via the alias
findings.finding: The `coach_listing` / `coach_instructions` function (coach.py:116, aliased as passes.coach_instructions) contains the hardcoded header '## Surviving advisory findings (by id)' (line 123). The plan mandates changing this header but attributes the fix ONLY to 'the hardcoded listing header in src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:123'. However, `plan_review_coach_inputs` calls `passes.coach_instructions` which is an alias of this same function — so widening `coach_inputs` to pass `coachable = blocking + surviving` while retaining the 'advisory' header in the listing is a pink-elephant antipattern: the prompt the LLM receives will label blocking findings as 'advisory'. The plan does not make explicit that fixing coach.py:123 resolves BOTH the workflow path (via coach_inputs) AND the bespoke path (via pass4_coach).
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:123; passes.py:74 (coach_instructions = _coach.coach_listing); plan 'What': 'the hardcoded listing header … in src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:123 becomes decision-neutral'
scenarios: Implementer fixes coach.py:123 correctly; since it is an alias, both paths are fixed — but only if they verify this alias relationship
Implementer adds a separate fix in workflow_ops.py thinking the alias is insufficient, creating a second copy that then diverges
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical: '(This single fix propagates to both the workflow path via the passes.coach_instructions alias and the bespoke pass4_coach path — no second copy needed.)'

findings.finding: The `plan_review_coach_inputs_output` JSON Schema (src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_coach_inputs_output.schema.json) declares `"additionalProperties": false` with only `{plan, instructions}` in `properties`. The plan adds a new `findings` output field to this step but does not name the schema file as an edit target — emitting `findings` from the op will fail schema validation at runtime.
findings.location: What / plan_review_coach_inputs bullet; src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_coach_inputs_output.schema.json
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"findings": { "type": "array", "description": "The combined coachable findings (blocking + surviving)." }` to the schema's `properties` and remove or relax `additionalProperties: false`.

findings.finding: The `plan_review_coach_inputs_output` schema is closed (`additionalProperties: false`) and declares only `{plan, instructions}`, but the plan adds a new `findings` output the workflow binds to `coach_gate.when` and `coach_notes.surviving`. No schema update is specified, so an executor adding `findings` to the op's return dict will trigger output-schema validation failure at runtime.
findings.location: src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_coach_inputs_output.schema.json / What section (plan_review_coach_inputs step changes)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"findings": { "type": "array", "description": "Blocking + surviving findings (blocking first) fed into the coach prompt and coach_gate trigger." }` to `plan_review_coach_inputs_output.schema.json` and set `"additionalProperties": true`.

findings.finding: The `plan-review-coach.md` prompt and its `with:` binding currently use `surviving` as the field name for findings passed to the LLM. The plan changes the YAML binding to `${{ steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings }}`, which maps the new combined findings list to a key named `findings` — but the prompt and its input contract still expect the key `surviving`. This key-name mismatch is an unenumerated edit target.
findings.location: What / plan-review.yaml bullet; src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml lines 195 and 273; src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_coach.md
findings.suggested_fix: Explicitly state the YAML binding becomes `surviving: ${{ steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings }}` (key unchanged, value changed) to avoid prompt-contract churn; or enumerate the prompt input contract as an edit target.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
